key_insight: The current mesh-relay's last-write-wins semantics is the single biggest gap; replacing it with CRDTs and causal ordering transforms this from a CRUD demo into a genuine distributed systems portfolio piece that maps directly to Lattice's core replication problem.

findings.description: Hybrid Logical Clocks + Component-Level CRDTs -- Replace the current last-write-wins Update in store.go with per-component versioning using Hybrid Logical Clocks (HLC). Each component gets an HLC timestamp; on merge, the store keeps the component with the higher HLC. This is the single most interview-relevant enhancement because it directly addresses how Lattice handles concurrent updates from multiple sensors at the edge after network partitions. The current mesh relay blindly overwrites with whatever arrives last, which an interviewer would immediately flag. Implementing HLCs (physical clock + logical counter) is roughly 150 lines and demonstrates you understand why wall clocks are insufficient in distributed systems. Add a VersionVector to Entity proto, implement HLC in a new internal/hlc package, and modify Store.Update to do component-level merge instead of full-entity replace. Complexity: M. Priority: 1. K8s analogy: ResourceVersion + conflict detection on kube-apiserver, but here you must also MERGE rather than just reject. Interview question: 'How would you handle two sensors updating the same entity while network-partitioned, then reconciling when connectivity returns?'
findings.recommendation: Add internal/hlc package with HLC implementation. Add version_vector field to Entity proto (map<string, uint64> where key is node ID). Modify Store.Update to compare HLC per component key and keep the winner. Modify mesh relay to propagate version vectors. Write tests that simulate concurrent updates from two stores and verify deterministic merge.

findings.description: Multi-Sensor Track Correlation / Fusion -- Add a second sensor type (e.g., radar-sim producing noisy position-only observations without velocity) and build an internal/fusion package that correlates observations from multiple sensors into a single fused track entity. Use a simple nearest-neighbor gate (position distance < threshold within time window) for association, and a weighted average for state estimation. This is the hardest thing to learn on the job because it requires understanding both the algorithmic side (association, gating) and the systems side (how do you avoid duplicate entities when two sensors see the same aircraft). The current system has a 1:1 sensor-to-track mapping which is unrealistic. Complexity: L. Priority: 2. K8s analogy: Like having multiple controllers that can create the same resource, and needing a finalizer/owner-reference pattern to deduplicate. Interview question: 'How would you design a system where multiple sensors produce observations of the same physical object, and you need to maintain a single fused track?'
findings.location: New cmd/radar-sim, new internal/fusion, touches internal/store for correlation index
findings.recommendation: Create cmd/radar-sim that produces noisier position-only observations as ENTITY_TYPE_TRACK with a 'source' component. Build internal/fusion service that watches all tracks, maintains a correlation matrix (track-pair distances), and merges correlated tracks into a single fused entity with provenance metadata showing contributing sensors. Add FusionComponent to proto with source_ids and fused_position.

findings.description: Partition Tolerance Integration Test Harness -- Build a test harness (internal/chaos or test/partition) that can programmatically introduce network partitions between mesh-relay peers, run both sides independently, then heal the partition and verify convergence. This is more valuable than any feature because it proves your distributed system actually works under failure. The current test suite is all happy-path. Use Go's net.Listener wrapping to inject a proxy that can drop/delay packets. Complexity: M. Priority: 2. K8s analogy: Like running a Jepsen-style test against etcd, or the way K8s integration tests use fake clocks and injected failures. Interview question: 'How would you test that your distributed system converges after a network partition? What invariants would you check?'
findings.recommendation: Build a test that spins up 3 entity-stores with mesh-relays, creates entities on store-A, partitions store-B from A and C, creates conflicting updates on both sides, heals partition, then asserts all three stores converge to identical state within a timeout. Use a controllable proxy (Go net.Conn wrapper) rather than iptables. This pairs perfectly with the HLC/CRDT enhancement.

findings.description: Human-on-the-Loop Approval Gate -- Add an approval gate between the task-manager's threat assessment and the actual task execution. When threat level escalates to HIGH (intercept), instead of immediately assigning tasks, transition to a 'pending_approval' state and expose a gRPC ApproveAction / DenyAction RPC. Include a configurable timeout that auto-denies if no human responds. This demonstrates understanding of autonomy levels and the critical safety constraint in military C2: a human must authorize lethal or high-consequence actions. It is a small code change with enormous interview signal. Complexity: S. Priority: 3. K8s analogy: Like an admission webhook that can block or mutate a resource before it is persisted, or like a PodDisruptionBudget that gates automated action. Interview question: 'How do you design an autonomous system that still keeps a human in the loop for high-consequence decisions, especially under time pressure?'
findings.recommendation: Add ApprovalState enum (auto_approved, pending, approved, denied, timed_out) to task assignment. For THREAT_LEVEL_HIGH, set state to pending and start a timer. Add ApproveAction/DenyAction RPCs to the store service. Extend lattice-cli with an 'approve' command. Write tests covering the timeout path and the race between approval and threat de-escalation.

findings.description: Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry -- Instrument the full entity lifecycle (sensor creates track -> store persists -> watcher notifies classifier -> classifier updates -> task-manager assigns) with OpenTelemetry trace context propagation through gRPC metadata. Add a trace_id to entity events so you can see the full causal chain from sensor observation to task assignment. This is not just observability theater -- the trace_id carried through gRPC metadata is essentially a correlation ID, and understanding how to propagate context across service boundaries via metadata is directly applicable to Lattice's architecture. Complexity: S. Priority: 4. K8s analogy: Like tracing a request through the API server -> etcd -> controller -> scheduler -> kubelet chain. Interview question: 'How do you debug latency or correctness issues in a distributed pipeline where data flows through multiple services asynchronously?'
findings.location: All cmd/ binaries, internal/server, gRPC interceptors
findings.recommendation: Add go.opentelemetry.io/otel dependencies. Create internal/telemetry package with tracer initialization (OTLP exporter to stdout or Jaeger). Add gRPC unary and stream interceptors for trace propagation. Instrument Store.Create/Update to start spans. Show a sample trace in the README. Keep it opt-in via OTEL_ENABLED env var so it doesn't complicate the default build.

findings.description: Gossip-Based Peer Discovery and Anti-Entropy -- Replace the static peer list in mesh-relay with a gossip protocol (SWIM or simplified protocol) for peer discovery, and add an anti-entropy background sync that periodically compares entity checksums between peers and repairs divergence. The current relay only forwards new events -- if a relay starts after entities exist, or if a forwarded event is lost, the peer will have a permanently incomplete view. Anti-entropy fixes this. Complexity: L. Priority: 4. K8s analogy: Like how etcd members discover each other and how the watch-cache replays missed events on reconnection (list-then-watch pattern). The anti-entropy is like etcd's snapshot + incremental approach. Interview question: 'Your mesh relay missed some events during a network blip. How do you detect and repair the inconsistency without re-transmitting everything?'
findings.recommendation: Implement a Merkle tree over entity IDs+versions. Periodically exchange tree roots between peers; walk the tree to find divergent subtrees; sync only those entities. For discovery, implement a simple UDP gossip protocol where peers exchange their known peer lists. This pairs well with HLC since version comparison becomes trivial.

findings.description: Edge-Degraded Mode with Bandwidth Budgeting -- Add a configurable bandwidth budget to the mesh relay that limits how many bytes/sec it will send to each peer. When over budget, implement priority-based queuing: HIGH threat entity updates go first, position updates are decimated (send every Nth), and classification updates are batched. Add a 'degraded' status that the CLI can display. This demonstrates understanding of the real constraints at the tactical edge: satellite links with limited bandwidth, and the need to prioritize what data gets through. Complexity: M. Priority: 3. K8s analogy: Like K8s resource quotas and priority classes for pod scheduling, but applied to network bandwidth. Also similar to how the API server rate-limits watch events. Interview question: 'You have a 64kbps satellite link connecting edge nodes. How do you ensure the most critical data gets through first?'
findings.recommendation: Add a priority queue in the relay between receiving local events and forwarding to peers. Assign priority based on entity threat level and event type (deletes > creates > updates for HIGH threat; updates can be coalesced for LOW). Add a token bucket rate limiter parameterized by bytes/sec. Add metrics: queue depth, drop rate, bytes sent per priority level. Test with a 1KB/s budget and verify HIGH threat entities always get through.

recommendation: Build in this order: (1) HLC + Component CRDTs first -- it is the foundation everything else builds on and the single highest-signal interview topic. (2) Partition tolerance tests -- proves the CRDTs actually work and shows testing maturity. (3) Human-on-the-loop approval gate -- small effort, huge Anduril-specific signal about autonomy safety. (4) Edge bandwidth budgeting -- pairs well with the mesh relay you just upgraded with CRDTs. (5) Multi-sensor fusion -- the largest effort but demonstrates the deepest domain knowledge. (6) Distributed tracing -- sprinkle in as you build the others, since it helps you debug. (7) Gossip + anti-entropy -- only if you have time; it is impressive but complex and the partition tests already demonstrate the key concepts. Skip fusion if time-constrained; the first four enhancements alone would make a very strong portfolio piece.
